perturbation.original_text: (b) Subject to the terms and conditions of this Section 17.3, each party will pay all properly invoiced fees within [***] after its receipt of a proper invoice therefor. All payments hereunder will be invoiced in U.S. Dollars. All payments hereunder will be made by wire transfer to the account specified by each Party; provided that a Party shall provide at least [***] advance notice of any changes to its account.
perturbation.changed_text: (b) Subject to the terms and conditions of this Section 17.3, each party will pay all properly invoiced fees within 90 days after its receipt of a proper invoice therefor. All payments hereunder will be invoiced in U.S. Dollars. All payments hereunder will be made by wire transfer to the account specified by each Party; provided that a Party shall provide at least 5 days advance notice of any changes to its account.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms to 90 days could create issues with vendors if the vendors operate in states with prompt payment laws that mandate shorter payment periods (e.g., for construction projects). Reducing the advance notice of account changes also presents legal risks.
perturbation.law_explanation: 815 ILCS 505/2 refers to the Illinois Consumer Fraud and Deceptive Business Practices Act and while it doesn't directly dictate payment terms between businesses, unreasonably extended payment terms (90 days) combined with a short notice for account changes (5 days), could be interpreted as an unfair or deceptive practice, particularly if it deviates significantly from industry standards and harms the vendor. Many industries have customary payment terms within 30-60 days, so a 90-day requirement could potentially be seen as exploitative, particularly towards smaller businesses. The short notice for bank account changes increases the risk of misdirected payments and disputes.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Illinois General Assembly - Illinois Compiled Statutes Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes ILCS Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. 815 ILCS 505/2 (815 ILCS 505/2) (from Ch. 121 1/2, par. 262) Sec. 2. Unfair methods of competition and unfair or deceptive acts or practices, including but not limited to the use or employment of any deception fraud, false pretense, false promise, misrepresentation or the concealment, suppression or omission of any material fact, with intent that others rely upon the concealment, suppression or omission of such material fact, or the use or employment of any practice described in Section 2 of the "Uniform Deceptive Trade Practices Act", approved August 5, 1965, in the conduct of any trade or commerce are hereby declared unlawful whether any person has in fact been misled, deceived or damaged thereby. In construing this section consideration shall be given to the interpretations of the Federal Trade Commission and the federal courts relating to Section 5 (a) of the Federal Trade Commission Act. (Source: P.A. 78-904 .) Home | Legislation & Laws | House | Senate | My Legislation | Disclaimers | Email This site is maintained for the Illinois General Assembly by the Legislative Information System, 705 Stratton Building, Springfield, Illinois 62706 Contact ILGA Webmaster
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the payment terms to 90 days and the advance notice for account changes to 5 days. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest that these changes could violate prompt payment laws and be considered unfair business practices, respectively. While the provided law snippet from 815 ILCS 505/2 doesn't directly specify payment terms, it broadly prohibits "unfair or deceptive acts or practices" in trade and commerce. The 90-day payment term, combined with the short notice for account changes, could be interpreted as such a practice, particularly if it deviates significantly from industry standards and harms the vendor.

perturbation.original_text: 14.4 Non-Solicitation. During the term of this Agreement and for a period of [***] thereafter, neither Party nor its controlled Affiliates will, without the prior written consent of the other Party, directly or indirectly solicit for employment any then-current employee of the other Party or its controlled Affiliates; [***].
perturbation.changed_text: 14.4 Non-Solicitation. During the term of this Agreement and for a period of ten (10) years thereafter, neither Party nor its controlled Affiliates will, without the prior written consent of the other Party, directly or indirectly solicit for employment any then-current employee of the other Party or its controlled Affiliates; [***].
perturbation.explanation: Extending the non-solicitation period to 10 years is likely unenforceable under California law, which strongly disfavors non-compete agreements and restrictions on employee mobility. This creates a legal contradiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_citation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 16600 states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." A 10-year non-solicitation agreement is likely to be considered an unreasonable restraint on trade, particularly in California, and therefore unenforceable. While it doesn't directly prevent someone from being employed, it restricts them from soliciting employees, limiting professional opportunities.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 16600 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 7. GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 2. PRESERVATION AND REGULATION OF COMPETITION [16600 - 17365] ( Part 2 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) CHAPTER 1. Contracts in Restraint of Trade [16600 - 16607] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 526. ) 16600. (a) Except as provided in this chapter, every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void. (b) (1) This section shall be read broadly, in accordance with Edwards v. Arthur Andersen LLP (2008) 44 Cal.4th 937, to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context, or any noncompete clause in an employment contract, no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter. (2) This subdivision does not constitute a change in, but is declaratory of, existing law. (c) This section shall not be limited to contracts where the person being restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business is a party to the contract. (Amended by Stats. 2023, Ch. 828, Sec. 1. (AB 1076) Effective January 1, 2024.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the non-solicitation period to ten years, which contradicts California Business and Professions Code Section 16600. The law states that "every contract by which anyone is restrained from engaging in a lawful profession, trade, or business of any kind is to that extent void." Extending the non-solicitation agreement to 10 years is an unreasonable restraint on trade under California law, making the contractual term unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that "This section shall be read broadly...to void the application of any noncompete agreement in an employment context...no matter how narrowly tailored, that does not satisfy an exception in this chapter." The extended period creates a clear non-compliance with the law.

perturbation.original_text: 25.2 Termination for Cause. Either Party may terminate this Agreement, immediately upon written notice to the other Party, if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and such breach (a) is incapable of cure or (b) being capable of cure, remains uncured [***] after the breaching Party receives written notice from the non-breaching Party thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: 25.2 Termination for Cause. Either Party may terminate this Agreement, immediately upon written notice to the other Party, if the other Party materially breaches this Agreement and such breach (a) is incapable of cure or (b) being capable of cure, remains uncured five (5) days after the breaching Party receives written notice from the non-breaching Party thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Providing only five days to cure a material breach of contract may be deemed unreasonable in many jurisdictions, particularly for complex commercial agreements. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in most contracts.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly addressing termination for cause, UCC § 2-609, concerning the right to adequate assurance of performance, implies a standard of reasonableness in commercial dealings. Giving only five days to cure a material breach, especially one involving complex performance obligations, may be deemed commercially unreasonable, violating the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Courts often look to industry standards and the complexity of the breach when determining the reasonableness of a cure period. Five days may be insufficient in many cases.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Uniform Commercial Code | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Uniform Commercial Code The Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) is a comprehensive set of laws governing commercial transactions, adopted in some form by every state and the District of Columbia. It aims to provide uniformity and consistency across states’ commercial laws . The UCC covers various aspects of commerce , including the sale and lease of goods , negotiable instruments , secured transactions , as well as on other categories related to banks and commercial transactions . See also: Commercial Law [Last reviewed in July of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities business law commercial law wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The altered text reduces the cure period for a material breach to five days, potentially contradicting the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. The Law Explanation argues this may be commercially unreasonable, especially for complex breaches, and that courts consider industry standards and breach complexity when determining reasonableness. While Scraped Law Snippet 2 generally defines the UCC's aim for uniformity in commercial laws, the Law Explanation uses UCC § 2-609 as an analogy, implying a need for 'adequate assurance of performance,' which a five-day cure period might not provide. This falls under 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction' because the time-sensitive obligation (cure period) is likely legally insufficient.
